groups.name: Fleabag Universe
groups.explanation: Fleabag (show title/character name), Phoebe Waller-Bridge (creator/actor), The Priest (character played by Andrew Scott), and 'This is a love story' (the show's closing line/tagline) are all from the BBC/Amazon series Fleabag.

groups.name: TRAP — Whodunit Mystery Titles (But One Is a TV Series, Not a Film)
groups.explanation: Knives Out and Glass Onion are theatrical films; Only Murders in the Building is a Hulu TV series; Poker Face is a Peacock TV series. All four share the whodunit mystery genre and are associated with Rian Johnson or share his aesthetic DNA, but players may assume all four are films — in fact two are movies and two are TV shows, making them a trap for anyone grouping by 'Rian Johnson Knives Out universe' (Poker Face and Only Murders are NOT Rian Johnson properties).

groups.name: Long-Suffering Wives of TV Antiheroes
groups.explanation: Betty Draper (wife of Don Draper in Mad Men), Skyler White (wife of Walter White in Breaking Bad), Carmela Soprano (wife of Tony Soprano in The Sopranos), and Lois Wilkerson (wife of Hal in Malcolm in the Middle) are all wives of complex, morally compromised male protagonists — women who are frequently criticized by audiences for 'getting in the way' of the antihero, a noted TV trope.

groups.name: Critically Acclaimed Cable Shows That Ended Before Their Time on Cinemax or Sundance
groups.explanation: Banshee (Cinemax), Orphan Black (BBC America), Rectify (Sundance Channel), and Halt and Catch Fire (AMC) are all critically acclaimed cult cable dramas from roughly 2012–2017 that were praised by critics as criminally underseen, frequently appeared on 'most underrated shows' lists, and ended with modest but devoted fanbases — none of them ever became mainstream hits despite being considered some of the best TV of their era.
